item.description:
Groovyday 0.3.7 — one scene, any size

  • Your selected visualizer now follows you across Compact, Floating, Desktop, and Fullscreen; changing mode expands or contracts the same scene
  • Appearance settings now contain one clear Visualizer picker instead of four mode-specific choices
  • Existing preferences migrate from the last-used mode, with the Floating selection preserved as the fallback

Groovyday 0.3.6 — particles that hear the music

  • Particles now maps bass to coherent near-field travel, mids to bending middle lanes, and treble to fine distant motion; the quiet baseline is calmer and less fuzzy
  • Strong hits launch two prominent streaking travelers, while secondary transients add smaller traffic—music changes motion and density, not just brightness
  • Desktop mode now uses Finder's real desktop-icon boundary on current macOS, fixing the window being placed nineteen layers beneath the wallpaper

Groovyday 0.3.5 — color placement, not color mixing

  • Single-hue covers now keep their strongest native tone and gain one restrained split-complementary family; the reported earth-tone cover resolves to warm gold with muted slate blue
  • Waves places supporting and counterpoint colors in separate depths and moving materials instead of averaging them into olive or beige
  • Ambient, Bars, Halo, and Particles also use distinct color regions or populations, preserving coordinated contrast throughout motion

Groovyday 0.3.4 — album colors, art directed

  • Every visualizer now receives an editorial three-tone palette: the cover's visual ground, a substantial supporting tone, and an intentional counterpoint
  • Counterpoints are selected by presence, hue relationship, value, and restrained chroma—tiny logo colors and fluorescent pixels can no longer dictate the scene
  • Two-tone covers preserve their real contrasting color; monochrome covers receive a quiet, deterministic harmony instead of random RGB color

Groovyday 0.3.3 — a universe in motion

  • Particles no longer collapse into a central gravity well: stars drift through layered orbital currents and palette-tinted nebulae, while beats launch travelers that cross the sky
  • Mode and Visualizer controls remain visible in Compact and Floating modes, including during silence and when track info is hidden
  • Desktop mode explicitly re-orders above Finder's wallpaper after switching, fixing cases where the mode changed but the visualizer did not appear

Groovyday 0.3.2 — a fuller sea

  • Sub-waves: smaller, faster crests spawned by secondary sounds — mid/treble transients and hits that arrive while the main breakers are busy. Dense music fills the sea with layered cross-motion; sparse music stays calm
  • The waterline is no longer a line: noise-eroded edges, feathered contact, and a mist band of detached droplets above every surface

Groovyday 0.3.1

  • "Show Track Info" now means what it says: with the toggle on, the info bar stays visible in floating mode (like lyrics) instead of hiding when the cursor leaves; with it off, hovering still reveals it temporarily

Groovyday 0.3.0 — the deep sea

  • Waves is no longer solid: five translucent wave trains stack like glass — you see the deeper sea through every crest, with subsurface glow, sinking light shafts, and inter-layer shading
  • Each depth layer listens to its own frequency register — treble shimmers far out, mids breathe in the middle distance, bass moves the near water — a stacked spectrum, like a MIDI visualizer made of ocean

Groovyday 0.2.9 — Waves refined

  • Breakers now travel the full width of the sea, melting away only at the far edge
  • Sun disc replaced with a wide, breathing horizon light
  • Spray is finer and falls under gravity instead of fountaining uniformly
  • Softer shading with per-wave grain texture — every wave looks different; the sea keeps rolling on its own even in silence, and sound hits it like wind

Groovyday 0.2.8

  • Desktop mode works. Two stacked bugs fixed: an invisible modal dialog was freezing the app when entering desktop mode, and the display-link API failing silently on macOS 26 meant no frames were drawn — rendering now has a guaranteed fallback pacer
  • Waves rebuilt from scratch: three parallax wave trains roll continuously; breakers spawn on drum hits, travel across the frame and decay — no more centered mound
  • Desktop-mode explainer is now a small non-blocking panel

Groovyday 0.2.7

  • Waves is now a surfer's wave: a breaking wave with a bass-driven barrel curl, foam lip, Hokusai-style face ribs, and spray flying off the crest on drum hits — same featherweight GPU cost

Groovyday 0.2.6

  • Desktop mode actually renders now — the visualizer paints your wallpaper as designed (an occlusion bug paused it forever)
  • Fullscreen hides the menu bar for distraction-free listening; slide the cursor to the top edge to reveal it
  • Overlay text and controls are always white on dark scrims — readable on any artwork or scene

Groovyday 0.2.5

  • New visualizer: Waves — a palette-tinted seascape. Bass drives the swell, drums send whitecap crashes rolling in from the horizon, treble sparkles on the water. As light on the GPU as Ambient.
  • Hover controls are now clearly visible on any artwork (white icons on dark chips)

Groovyday 0.2.4

  • Never get stuck in Desktop mode again: reopening Groovyday from Finder/Dock always returns to Floating, and cold launches no longer restore Desktop implicitly
  • One-time explainer when entering Desktop mode listing every way back (hotkeys ⌃⌥2 / ⌃⌥Space, menu bar, reopen)
  • Right-click anywhere for the full app menu, with lyrics and track-info toggles per mode

Groovyday 0.2.3

  • Right-click anywhere on the visualizer for the full app menu — modes, visualizer, source, lyrics on/off, track info on/off, Settings, updates, Quit
  • Lyrics and track-info toggles for the current mode, right in the menu
  • Update checks run at every launch

Groovyday 0.2.2

  • Update checks now run at every launch (previously only once a day), so new releases are noticed right away
  • Full app menu inside the window: hover the mode-switcher or right-click the visualizer for Settings, Check for Updates, and Quit — works even when the menu bar icon is hidden by the MacBook notch

Groovyday 0.2.1

  • Full app menu now available from the window itself: hover the visualizer and open the mode switcher (Settings, Check for Updates, Quit), or right-click anywhere
  • Fixes the app being unreachable when the menu bar icon is hidden (e.g. next to the MacBook notch)

Groovyday 0.2.0

  • First public build: five visualizers (Bars, Halo, Ambient, Particles, Skyline)
  • Time-synced lyrics with per-mode toggles and timing control
  • Four window modes with animated transitions, global hotkeys
  • Music & Spotify metadata, transport, and scrubbing
